design_philosophy.core_loop: Combat-focused platforming with resource management: defeat enemies to earn currency for shop purchases, manage limited health pools across five stages, summon companion Zap for tactical advantage
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity balanced against progression needs. Players must choose between purchasing temporary power-ups or accumulating currency; limited health management without frequent checkpoints creates attrition pressure
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Reclaim a stolen kingdom as an exile prince with supernatural aid. Asymmetric partnership with a loyal dog companion who provides both combat support and emotional narrative anchoring
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Mechanically unique companion system (Zap) predates similar mechanics like Little Red Riding Hood. Character-driven narrative in era of minimal storytelling. Currency-based progression grants player agency in how to tackle challenges. Japanese aesthetic and design retained even in US localization suggests intentional artistic vision

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives with game-over state; debug mode allows extra lives up to 99
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression; no mid-stage checkpoints documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: three crystal choices at start (Flight for mobility, Life for durability, Spirit for damage) fundamentally alter difficulty approach. Power-ups and scrolls purchasable from Kim's shop allow customization. US version reduced overall difficulty compared to Japanese original and added health restoration cheat code

progression.upgrade_system: Three initial crystal selections granting permanent passive effects. Temporary power-ups (replicating crystal effects) dropped by enemies or purchased. Scrolls provide long-range weapon alternatives. Moon Mirror obtained by rescuing Crystal Princess
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage completion gates advancement through five themed realms. Power-up availability tied to enemy defeats and shop inventory
replayability_hooks: Three mutually exclusive crystal choices create different difficulty curves and playstyle variations
Resource management decisions (spend gold on temporary buffs vs. save for key items) offer strategic replay value
Hidden debug mode and sound test reward player exploration

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling: five thematically distinct realms (Stage 3 'Realm of Hungry Ghosts' especially evocative)
NPC dialogue: Zap provides exposition and encouragement; Kim acts as hint-giver and news reporter
Introductory exposition: backstory of kingdom conquest delivered by Zap before gameplay begins
Character design: Japanese naming and aesthetic (retaining kanji on stage names and HUD) establishes Far Eastern fantasy setting
Item design: magical crystals as narrative-mechanical integration (Crystal Princess reward system)

faction_indicators: Farron (player) + Zap + Kim + Crystal Princess: liberation coalition
Five thematic realms suggest conquered territories under Zaras rule
danger_signals: Ghost enemies (Stage 3) and 'bizarre enemies' throughout realms indicate supernatural corruption of kingdom
Kidnapped Crystal Princess signals political imprisonment and control
Demonic/magical nature of antagonist Zaras (魔 'ma' - magic/demon character in Japanese naming)
environmental_storytelling: Stage 3 'Realm of Hungry Ghosts' visual transformation between Japanese (Edvard Munch-inspired baby heads) and US versions (biomass writhing) - suggests psychological horror of conquered realm
Five distinct perilous realms convey scale of kingdom's corruption
Shop presence (Kim's store) suggests surviving settlements and commerce under occupation

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Conquest of the Crystal Palace (regional differences, debug mode, design features)
Wikipedia - Conquest of the Crystal Palace (gameplay mechanics, story, development context, US difficulty reduction)
